steps.text: Provide an educational program to familiarize employees with the general principles of fire extinguisher use and the hazards involved with incipient stage firefighting.
Provide the education required in paragraph (g)(1) upon initial employment and at least annually thereafter.
Provide employees who have been designated to use fire fighting equipment training in the use of the appropriate equipment.
Provide the training required in paragraph (g)(3) upon initial assignment to the designated group of employees and at least annually thereafter.
Provide training in the various precautions and safe practices described in this section.
Certify that employees have been trained by preparing a certification record.
Include subjects such as recognition and avoidance of dangers, emergency procedures, and first-aid training in the training.
Ensure only persons trained in the operation of the derrick operate the derrick.
Instruct employees involved in using high voltages to locate trouble or test cables in necessary precautions.
Provide medical services and first aid as required in Section 1910.151.
Ensure employee proficiency in the work practices required by this section.
Introduce the procedures necessary for compliance with this section.
Certify that each employee has received the training required by paragraph (a)(2).
Maintain certification for the duration of the employee’s employment.
Document employee training in records.
Conduct cardiopulmonary resuscitation and first-aid training.
Train each employee who is subject to exposure to inorganic arsenic above the action level.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation.
Provide training program by October 1, 1978, for covered employees at the time of initial assignment and annually thereafter.
Inform each employee of the information contained in Appendix A.
Inform employees of the quantity, location, manner of use, storage, sources of exposure, and necessary protective steps related to inorganic arsenic.
Inform employees about the purpose, proper use, and limitations of respirators.
Describe the medical surveillance program as required.
Inform about engineering controls and work practices associated with the employee’s job assignment.
Conduct a review of the standard.
Make readily available a copy of this standard and its appendices to all affected employees.
Provide all materials relating to the employee information and training program upon request.
Provide additional information on hazards of exposure and engineering and work practice controls.
Inform employees if they are exposed over the permissible exposure limit and actions to reduce exposures.
Conduct training session covering signs, labels, and color coding.
Provide an opportunity for interactive questions and answers.
Ensure the trainer is knowledgeable in the subject matter.
Provide additional training for employees in HIV and HBV laboratories.
Assure employees demonstrate proficiency in standard microbiological practices before working with HIV or HBV.
Ensure employees have prior experience in handling human pathogens before working with HIV or HBV.
Provide a training program for employees with no prior experience in handling human pathogens.
Assign a progression of work activities as techniques are learned.
Assure employees participate in work activities involving infectious agents only after proficiency has been demonstrated.
Maintain training records for 3 years.
Make training records available upon request.
Provide employee training records upon request.
Train each employee in the applicable requirements of this section no later than October 31, 2011.
Train each employee who is, or may be, in an area where lockout/tags-plus systems are being used.
Educate employees about the purpose and function of the employer’s lockout/tags-plus program and procedures.
Inform employees about the unique identity of the locks and tags to be used in the lockout/tags-plus system.
Explain the basic components of the tags-plus system: an energy-isolating device with a tag affixed to it and an additional safety measure.
Prohibit tampering with or removing any lockout/tags-plus system.
Prohibit restarting or reenergizing any machinery, equipment, or system being serviced under a lockout/tags-plus system.
Train each employee who is likely to be exposed in excess of a PEL.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation.
Provide training prior to or at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.
Ensure training for Class I operations is equivalent to the EPA Model Accreditation Plan.
Provide training for Class II operations that require critical barriers and/or negative pressure enclosures.
Include training elements for work with asbestos containing materials.
Include 'hands-on' training for courses related to asbestos work and ensure it takes at least 8 hours.

Before using any fall protection system, the employee shall be trained in the use and application limits of the equipment, proper hook-up, anchoring and tie-off techniques, methods of use, and proper methods of equipment inspection and storage.

Workers regularly on site who work in areas which have been monitored and fully characterized indicating that exposures are under permissible exposure limits and published exposure limits where respirators are not necessary, shall receive a minimum of 24 hours of instruction off the site and the minimum of one day actual field experience under the direct supervision of a trained, experienced supervisor.

Workers with 24 hours of training who are covered by paragraphs (e)(3)(ii) and (e)(3)(iii) of this section, and who become general site workers or who are required to wear respirators, shall have the additional 16 hours and two days of training necessary to total the training specified in paragraph (e)(3)(i).

On-site management and supervisors directly responsible for, or who supervise employees engaged in, hazardous waste operations shall receive 40 hours initial training, and three days of supervised field experience (the training may be reduced to 24 hours and one day if the only area of their responsibility is employees covered by paragraphs (e)(3)(ii) and (e)(3)(iii)) and at least eight additional hours of specialized training at the time of job assignment on such topics as, but not limited to, the employer’s safety and health program and the associated employee training program, personal protective equipment program, spill containment program, and health hazard monitoring procedure and techniques.

Trainers shall be qualified to instruct employees about the subject matter that is being presented in training. Such trainers shall have satisfactorily completed a training program for teaching the subjects they are expected to teach, or they shall have the academic credentials and instructional experience necessary for teaching the subjects. Instructors shall demonstrate competent instructional skills and knowledge of the applicable subject matter.

Employees and supervisors that have received and successfully completed the training and field experience specified in paragraphs (e)(1) through (e)(4) of this section shall be certified by their instructor or the head instructor and trained supervisor as having successfully completed the necessary training. A written certificate shall be given to each person so certified. Any person who has not been so certified or who does not meet the requirements of paragraph (e)(9) of this section shall be prohibited from engaging in hazardous waste operations.
Trainers who teach any of the above training subjects shall have satisfactorily completed a training course for teaching the subjects they are expected to teach.
Those employees who are trained in accordance with paragraph (q)(6) shall receive annual refresher training of sufficient content and duration to maintain their competencies.
A statement shall be made of the training or competency, and if a statement of competency is made, the employer shall keep a record of the methodology used to demonstrate competency.
Chemical protective clothing and equipment to be used by organized and designated HAZMAT team members shall meet the requirements of paragraphs (g)(3) through (5).

Upon completion of the emergency response, if it is determined that it is necessary to remove hazardous substances, the employer conducting the clean-up shall comply with one of the specified requirements.
Where the clean-up is done on plant property using plant or workplace employees, such employees shall have completed the training requirements of 29 CFR 1926.35, 1926.59, and 1926.103.
All equipment to be used in the performance of the clean-up work shall be in serviceable condition and shall have been inspected prior to use.
Each hazardous waste site clean-up effort will require an occupational safety and health program headed by the site coordinator or the employer’s representative.
The occupational safety and health program will need to be developed before work begins on the site and implemented as needed.
Address safety and health hazards employees should expect on hazardous waste clean-up sites.
Address effective control measures or techniques for those hazards.
Address effective monitoring procedures in characterizing exposure levels.
Address what makes an effective employer’s safety and health program.
Address what a site safety and health plan should include.
Provide hands-on training with personal protective equipment and clothing.
Address the contents of the OSHA standard relevant to the employee’s duties and function.
Address employee’s responsibilities under OSHA and other regulations.
Train supervisors in their responsibilities under the safety and health program.
Address the spill containment program in supervisor training.
Address the personal protective equipment program in supervisor training.
Address the medical surveillance program in supervisor training.
Address the emergency response plan in supervisor training.
Train employees on employer’s safety and health program elements impacting them.
Address the hazard communication program for employees.
Address the medical surveillance program for employees.
Train employees on hazards and controls necessary for their job duties.
Provide annual refresher training for employees.
Address competencies required for various levels of response for employees.
Address hazards associated with hazardous substances.
Address hazard identification and awareness.
Address notification of appropriate persons.
Address the need for and use of personal protective equipment including respirators.
Address the decontamination procedures to be used.
Address preplanning activities for hazardous substance incidents including the emergency response plan.
Address company standard operating procedures for hazardous substance emergency responses.
Address the use of the incident command system.
Conduct critiques after an incident including evaluation of what worked and what did not.
Train hazardous materials specialists in care and use of chemical protective clothing.
Train hazardous materials specialists on the medical surveillance program.
Train hazardous materials specialists on standard operating procedures for the hazardous materials team.
Provide training in the nature of the hazards associated with work within a controlled decking zone.
Establish, access, and implement proper installation techniques and work practices required by 1926.760(c) and 1926.754(e).
Develop a site-specific erection plan in accordance with 1926.752(e).
Provide certification for each employee who has received training for performing steel erection operations as required by 1926.761.
Instruct employees in the recognition and avoidance of hazards associated with underground construction activities.
Practice donning and using self-contained breathing apparatus monthly for rescue team members on jobsites with flammable or noxious gases.
Train each employee who is likely to be exposed in excess of a PEL.
Conduct training at no cost to the employee.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.
Provide training prior to or at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.
Provide training for Class I operations and for Class II operations that require the use of critical barriers and/or negative pressure enclosures.
Include hands-on training for work with asbestos containing roofing materials, flooring materials, siding materials, ceiling tiles, or transite panels; the course shall take at least 8 hours.

preconditions.text: Employers must ensure employees do not engage in activities until they have received proper training.
Where training is required, it shall consist of on-the-job training or classroom-type training or a combination of both.
Training need not be provided to employees already trained in required precautions and safe practices prior to employment.
If new technology, new types of equipment, or changes in procedures necessitate the use of safety-related work practices that are different from those normally used.
If the employee must employ safety-related work practices that are not normally used during regular job duties.
Before the performance of work practices involved, employees must have retraining if tasks are performed less often than once per year.

constraints.text: The certification record shall be prepared at the completion of training and maintained on file for the duration of the employee’s employment.
The certification record shall be made available upon request to the Assistant Secretary for Occupational Safety and Health.
